articles.body:
When H.E. Kamel El-Wazir walks into a room, the energy shifts. A former Commander of the Egyptian Armed Forces' Engineering Authority, he brings to his ministerial role the discipline of a military man and the vision of a builder. Under his direction, Egypt has launched seven international logistics corridors, accelerated port infrastructure across the Red Sea and Mediterranean, and begun positioning the country not merely as a transit route between East and West — but as a destination in its own right.

We met the Minister at his Cairo office, where blueprints and maps covered an entire wall. He spoke without notes, with the precision of someone who has memorised every kilometre of the national road network.

Egypt is not just a corridor. It has never been just a corridor. Our geography is our inheritance — but our infrastructure is our choice.
— H.E. Kamel El-Wazir, Minister of Transport and Industry

On Egypt's Infrastructure Moment

Logistics Magazine: Minister, Egypt is investing at a scale rarely seen in the region. What is driving this moment?

H.E. El-Wazir: The moment did not arrive by accident. We have been building toward it for years. What you see now — the Safaga 2 terminal, the Ismailia Yacht Marina, the seven international corridors — these are not separate projects. They are one integrated vision. Egypt sits at the intersection of three continents. If we do not capture that advantage now, someone else will build the infrastructure that should be ours.

LM: The Safaga-Qena-Abu Tartour corridor has received significant attention. Why is this project so strategically important?

El-Wazir: Because it connects the Red Sea to Upper Egypt and to the Golden Triangle mining zone in a single unbroken line. We are not just moving containers. We are unlocking an entire economic region that has been waiting decades for this connection. The phosphate reserves alone are worth more than people understand. Add the rail link, add the terminal, and you have a full logistics ecosystem — not a port, an ecosystem.

7 International Corridors
776,000 m² Safaga 2 Terminal Area
2M+ Containers/Year Capacity
17m Quay Depth

On the Suez Canal and Global Trade Disruption

LM: The Houthi situation in the Red Sea suppressed Suez Canal traffic significantly. How has Egypt responded?

El-Wazir: We responded by accelerating. When traffic fell, some people saw a crisis. We saw an instruction. The instruction was: do not depend on transit alone. Build the infrastructure that makes Egypt valuable regardless of what happens in the Bab el-Mandeb. We pushed forward on the Mediterranean ports, on inland connectivity, on the railway. So when the Red Sea reopens fully — and it will — Egypt will be ready at a level it was not at before.

LM: Do you see Egypt competing with Gulf logistics hubs — Dubai, Abu Dhabi — or complementing them?

El-Wazir: Complementing. This is not competition. The region is large enough for multiple centres of excellence. What Egypt offers is different — a manufacturing base, a labour force of 106 million people, a Suez Canal, a Mediterranean coastline, and now the infrastructure to connect all of it. Dubai moves goods. Egypt can move goods and make them. That is a different proposition entirely.

When traffic fell, some people saw a crisis. We saw an instruction. Build the infrastructure that makes Egypt valuable regardless of what happens elsewhere.
— H.E. Kamel El-Wazir

On the Next Generation

LM: What do you say to young Egyptians who want to build careers in logistics and maritime?

El-Wazir: I say: the timing has never been better. Egypt is building. It needs engineers, project managers, logistics specialists, maritime lawyers, port operators. The country is creating the infrastructure — but infrastructure without people is concrete and steel. We need the generation that will run these terminals, manage these corridors, negotiate these contracts. That generation is Egyptian. It should be trained in Egypt, employed in Egypt, and proud of what Egypt is building.

LM: Final question, Minister. If you could say one thing to the international logistics community about Egypt — one thing you wish they truly understood — what would it be?

El-Wazir: That we are serious. We have always had the geography. We have always had the ambition. What we have now, that perhaps we did not always have, is the execution. Come and see for yourself. The cranes are real. The terminals are real. The corridors are real. Egypt is open — and Egypt is ready.
articles.author: Logistics Magazine Editorial Team
articles.title: "Egypt is Not Just a Corridor — It is a Destination": H.E. Kamel El-Wazir on Infrastructure, Trade, and the Next Decade
articles.lead: Egypt's Minister of Transport and Industry, H.E. Kamel El-Wazir, sits at the centre of one of the most ambitious infrastructure transformations in the Arab world. In this exclusive interview, he speaks candidly about the Safaga corridor, Egypt's emerging role as a global logistics hub, and what the next decade demands from the region's leaders.
